stem: Let \( f(x,y) = [[A]]x^{2} + [[B]]xy + [[C]]y^{2} \). What is the directional derivative of \( f \) at \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) in the direction of the vector \( \langle [[a]], [[b]] \rangle \) (i.e. along the unit vector \( \hat{u} = \langle [[a]],[[b]] \rangle / |\langle [[a]],[[b]] \rangle| \))?
solution:
The gradient is \( 
abla f = (2\cdot[[A]]x+[[B]]y,\; [[B]]x+2\cdot[[C]]y) \), so \( 
abla f([[x0]],[[y0]]) = (2\cdot[[A]]\cdot[[x0]]+[[B]]\cdot[[y0]],\; [[B]]\cdot[[x0]]+2\cdot[[C]]\cdot[[y0]]) \). The unit vector is \( \hat u = \langle [[a]],[[b]]angle/[[norm]] \), and \( D_{\hat u}f = 
abla f\cdot\hat u = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( |\langle [[a]],[[b]]\rangle| \), using the raw (non-unit) vector instead.
This keeps only the \( f_x \) contribution to the dot product, dropping the \( f_y \) term entirely.
This keeps only the \( f_y \) contribution to the dot product, dropping the \( f_x \) term entirely.
This swaps the roles of \( a \) and \( b \) in the dot product.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x,y) = [[A]]x^{2}y + [[B]]xy^{2} + [[C]]x + [[D]]y \). What is the directional derivative of \( f \) at \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) in the direction of the (already unit) vector \( \langle [[ux]], [[uy]] \rangle \)?
solution: Since \( \langle [[ux]],[[uy]] \rangle \) is already a unit vector along a coordinate axis, the directional derivative reduces to a single partial derivative: \( f_x([[x0]],[[y0]]) = 2\cdot[[A]]\cdot[[x0]]\cdot[[y0]]+[[B]]\cdot[[y0]]^{2}+[[C]] \) and \( f_y([[x0]],[[y0]]) = [[A]]\cdot[[x0]]^{2}+2\cdot[[B]]\cdot[[x0]]\cdot[[y0]]+[[D]] \). Along \( \langle [[ux]],[[uy]]\rangle \), \( D_{\hat u}f = f_x\cdot([[ux]]) + f_y\cdot([[uy]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps which partial derivative pairs with which axis component.
This is \( f([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), the function value, not a directional derivative.
This reports \( f_x \) even when the direction is along the \( y \)-axis (or vice versa).
This reports \( f_y \) even when the direction is along the \( x \)-axis (or vice versa).
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x,y) = [[c]]x^{2} + [[d]]y^{2} \). What is the maximum possible rate of change of \( f \) (that is, \( |\nabla f| \)) at the point \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \)?
solution: The gradient is \( \nabla f([[x0]],[[y0]]) = (2\cdot[[c]]\cdot[[x0]],\; 2\cdot[[d]]\cdot[[y0]]) \). The maximum rate of change at this point is \( |\nabla f([[x0]],[[y0]])| = \sqrt{(2\cdot[[c]]\cdot[[x0]])^{2}+(2\cdot[[d]]\cdot[[y0]])^{2}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two partial derivatives instead of taking the Euclidean norm of the gradient.
This uses the sum of absolute values (a Manhattan-style norm) instead of the Euclidean norm.
This reports only the \( f_x \) component of the gradient.
This reports only the \( f_y \) component of the gradient.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x,y) = [[a]]\sin(x) + [[b]]y^{2} \). What is the directional derivative of \( f \) at \( (0,[[y0]]) \) in the direction of the unit vector \( \hat u = \left\langle \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[r]]}, \dfrac{[[q]]}{[[r]]} \right\rangle \)?
solution: The gradient is \( \nabla f(x,y) = (\cos(x)\cdot[[a]],\; 2\cdot[[b]]y) \), so \( \nabla f(0,[[y0]]) = ([[a]],\; 2\cdot[[b]]\cdot[[y0]]) \) since \( \cos(0)=1 \). Dotting with \( \hat u \) gives \( D_{\hat u}f = [[a]]\cdot\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[r]]} + 2\cdot[[b]]\cdot[[y0]]\cdot\dfrac{[[q]]}{[[r]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the \( p \) and \( q \) weights entirely, as if \( \hat u = \langle 1,1\rangle/r \).
This keeps only the \( f_x \) contribution, dropping the \( f_y \) term.
This keeps only the \( f_y \) contribution, dropping the \( f_x \) term.
This forgets the factor of 2 from differentiating \( y^{2} \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x,y) = [[c]]x^{2} + [[d]]y^{2} \). What is the directional derivative of \( f \) at \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) in the direction of steepest descent (the direction in which \( f \) decreases fastest)?
solution:
The direction of steepest descent is \( -
abla f/|
abla f| \), and the directional derivative in that direction is \( -|
abla f([[x0]],[[y0]])| \). Since \( 
abla f([[x0]],[[y0]]) = (2\cdot[[c]]\cdot[[x0]],\, 2\cdot[[d]]\cdot[[y0]]) \), the answer is \( -\sqrt{(2\cdot[[c]]\cdot[[x0]])^{2}+(2\cdot[[d]]\cdot[[y0]])^{2}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the negative sign; steepest DESCENT gives the negative of the gradient's magnitude.
This negates the sum of the partials instead of the Euclidean norm.
This reports only (the negative of) the \( f_x \) component.
This reports only (the negative of) the \( f_y \) component.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
